principle: Make contrast strong enough to be obviously intentional — a sort-of-heavy line next to a sort-of-heavier line looks like a mistake, not a design decision
explanation: X.ui|contrast_strength: wimpy_contrast: similar_weights+similar_sizes+similar_colors -> looks_like_mistake; effective_contrast: elements_must_be_VERY_different -> no_ambiguity_about_intention; rule: either_same_or_very_different; examples: hairline_vs_4pt_rule NOT hairline_vs_half_point; dark_brown_headline_on_black_body -> no_contrast; NO:dark_brown_on_black_text NO:two_typefaces_that_are_similar; eg: Times_headline+Times_body >> Aachen_Bold_headline+Times_body -> unambiguous_contrast

principle: Use contrast to serve both organization and visual interest simultaneously — a reader should glance at a page and instantly understand its hierarchy before reading a single word
explanation: X.ui|contrast_dual_purpose: two_purposes: (1)create_visual_interest -> page_more_likely_to_be_read (2)organize_information -> reader_understands_flow_instantly; inseparable: organized_page == visually_interesting; contrasting_elements: should_NEVER_confuse_reader; should_NEVER_create_false_focus_on_secondary_item; test: single_glance -> can_reader_understand_organization_and_flow?; NO:decorative_contrast_that_confuses_hierarchy NO:contrast_that_makes_secondary_item_look_primary; eg: resume_uniform_type >> bold_section_headers+contrasting_job_titles -> skimmable_hierarchy

principle: Set key phrases in bold within gray body text to create entry points — a completely gray page discourages casual readers, but bold phrases let eyes scan and pull readers into the full text
explanation: X.ui|bold_in_body: gray_page: no_contrast -> discourages_casual_reader; bold_phrases: create_visual_entry_points -> reader_scans_bold_first -> pulled_into_full_text; mechanism: lighter_commitment_to_scan -> reader_reads_bold -> bound_to_read_more; exception: novels+formal_journals: gray_page_intentional -> type_should_be_invisible; bold_sans_in_serif_body: go_1pt_smaller -> sans_appears_same_size; NO:gray_wall_of_text_no_entry_points NO:bolding_randomly_not_key_points; eg: medical_flyer_all_same_size >> bold_key_symptoms+bold_benefits -> entry_points

principle: Apply contrast last after proximity, alignment, and repetition — elements of contrast often double as elements of repetition and adding contrast tends to make the other principles fall into place
explanation: X.ui|four_principles_together: process_order: (1)proximity (2)alignment (3)repetition (4)contrast_last; adding_contrast: often_causes_other_principles_to_fall_into_place; dual_role: elements_of_contrast == elements_of_repetition; eg: bold_headline_style -> contrasts_with_body AND repeats_across_all_sections; never_use_just_one_principle -> they_interact; NO:adding_random_contrast_before_establishing_proximity+alignment NO:contrast_only_means_typefaces; eg: chaotic_ad >> group+align+repetition_first -> contrast_appears_naturally

principle: Find the most important element and make it the clear focal point with strong contrast — readers who see a clear focal point will follow the visual hierarchy you create from there
explanation: X.ui|contrast_focal_point: no_focal_point: reader_doesnt_know_where_to_start -> everything_equal_weight -> nothing_read; create_focal_point: most_interesting_OR_most_important_element -> emphasize_strongly_with_contrast; visual_hierarchy: strong_focal_point -> reader_enters -> follows_path; keep_together: most_important_items_near_focal_point; NO:making_everything_the_same_size NO:all_caps_centered_text_as_substitute_for_real_contrast; eg: all-caps_centered_pie_shop_ad >> large_pie_image+strong_shop_name+contrasting_list
